does: NeuroStrike presents a neuron-level attack framework that targets the safety mechanisms of aligned large language models by exploiting safety neurons—specialized, sparse neural components responsible for detecting and suppressing harmful content. In white-box settings, the method identifies these safety neurons through feedforward activation analysis and disables them via pruning during inference. In black-box settings, NeuroStrike introduces an LLM profiling attack that leverages the transferability of safety neurons across models, enabling attacks without direct model access. The framework demonstrates both high success rates and generalizability across different aligned models, revealing a fundamental architectural vulnerability in how current alignment techniques protect model behavior.
connects: This work builds on growing research into adversarial vulnerabilities of aligned language models, extending beyond prompt-based jailbreaking attacks that rely on trial-and-error and lack scalability. It connects to interpretability research on neural network components and their functional specialization, particularly studies identifying distinct neuron groups responsible for specific behaviors. NeuroStrike also draws on adversarial machine learning traditions of identifying and exploiting concentrated failure modes in neural networks. The paper responds to observations that supervised fine-tuning and RLHF-based alignment techniques create exploitable surface-level safety behaviors rather than robust value alignment, building on earlier findings about the brittleness of current alignment approaches.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing more robust alignment techniques that distribute safety mechanisms across broader neural circuits rather than relying on sparse, specialized neurons vulnerable to targeted intervention. It motivates research into mechanistic safety properties that are difficult to localize or disable through simple architectural manipulation. The neuron-level attack methodology enables future work on safety auditing and red-teaming at the component level, supporting systematic discovery of alignment vulnerabilities. Additionally, NeuroStrike's findings about safety neuron transferability could inform more thorough safety evaluation protocols and inspire defense mechanisms that either obfuscate neural safety mechanisms or implement redundancy patterns that resist neuron-level manipulation.
